components.title: GitLab (Source Code Management)
components.description: GitLab (self-managed or SaaS) providing source code version control, code review via merge requests, and CI/CD pipelines for the organisation's software development lifecycle. Branch protection rules require peer review and passing pipeline checks before merge to protected branches. CI/CD pipelines include automated static analysis (SAST), dependency vulnerability scanning, and secret scanning. Repository and pipeline audit events are forwarded to the SIEM.
components.purpose: Provide a secure source code management and CI/CD platform supporting the organisation's secure software development lifecycle, from code review through to automated build and deployment.

props.name: branch-protection
props.value: Protected branches require merge request approval and passing pipeline before merge; force-push disabled

props.name: pipeline-security-scanning
props.value: SAST, dependency scanning, and secret detection enabled in CI/CD pipelines

props.name: identity-platform
props.value: SSO via SAML/OIDC integrated with organisation identity provider, MFA enforced

props.name: artifact-signing
props.value: Build artifacts and container images signed as part of the release pipeline

protocols.name: https
protocols.title: HTTPS — Web UI, Git-over-HTTPS, API, and CI/CD runner communication

protocols.name: ssh
protocols.title: SSH — Git-over-SSH for developer clone/push/pull operations

protocols.name: syslog
protocols.title: Syslog — Application and audit event forwarding to SIEM

control-implementations.description: Control implementations for GitLab (Source Code Management).

implemented-requirements.description: Secure configuration guidance (a hardening/loosening guide) for GitLab instance configuration is produced and maintained, covering authentication settings, branch protection defaults, and CI/CD runner isolation, and made available to project maintainers as part of the organisation's secure development guidance.

Repository and project access in GitLab is restricted to only the developers and teams who require it for their work, using GitLab's project/group permission model (Guest through Owner roles), with access reviewed periodically to remove stale grants.

Multi-factor authentication is required for all users authenticating to GitLab, enforced via SSO integration with the organisation's identity provider (which enforces MFA) or GitLab's built-in two-factor authentication where SSO is not used.

CI/CD pipeline credentials (deployment keys, cloud provider credentials, API tokens) are stored as masked, protected GitLab CI/CD variables or in an integrated external secrets manager, never committed in plaintext to the repository. Secret scanning in the CI/CD pipeline detects and blocks any credentials accidentally committed to code.

Automated dependency vulnerability scanning is run on every pipeline execution, identifying known-vulnerable third-party libraries and packages before code is merged or deployed, with findings tracked through to remediation.
GitLab audit events (project access changes, permission changes, protected branch changes, pipeline execution, failed authentication attempts) are centrally logged and forwarded to the SIEM.
Where GitLab CI/CD is used to build software distributed to consumers, a software bill of materials (SBOM) is produced as part of the pipeline and made available to consumers of that software.
